narrative: As an Asian male applicant, your law school application was evaluated using a model trained on data from thousands of students who took the bar exam, focusing on factors like academic records, test scores, and background to predict first-time pass rates. The model assessed your profile— including your undergraduate GPA of 3.20, LSAT score of 33, middle family income bracket, part-time enrollment status, and school deciles of 5th in Year 1 and 3rd in Year 3—and predicted an 83% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first attempt. This high predicted failure risk is why your application was rejected, as the school prioritizes admitting students likely to succeed on the bar to maintain their strong pass rates. The three biggest factors pushing the model toward this "fail" prediction, in order of influence, were your planned part-time status, your lower Year 3 school decile ranking, and your middle family income bracket. Being in the part-time track, which only 7.3% of students choose compared to 92.7% full-time, had the strongest pull toward failure, as the model sees it as a major risk linked to divided focus and lower bar success in past data. Your 3rd-place decile in Year 3 grades—below the typical 5.53 average—reinforced this concern, signaling a dip in performance that often correlates with bar struggles. Your middle income level added a smaller but still negative nudge, as students from this bracket have shown slightly higher failure rates in the dataset. While your undergraduate GPA sat right around the average of 3.21 and your LSAT was close to typical ranges, these weren't enough to counterbalance the top concerns. Compared to students who typically get approved, who often have stronger late-stage grades, full-time commitments, and profiles pushing toward bar passage, your combination stood out as riskier. This doesn't reflect your full potential or effort, but rather patterns the model has learned from real outcomes. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT for a higher score, strengthening your recent academic decile through targeted coursework, exploring full-time options if feasible, and gathering letters highlighting your resilience as an Asian male navigating these challenges. With adjustments like these, you could reapply stronger to another program or build experience elsewhere to boost your bar readiness.
